questions.question: What command lists all files in the current directory?
options: cd
ls (Mac/Linux) or dir (Windows)
mkdir
pwd
questions.explanation: ls (Mac/Linux) and dir (Windows) list directory contents. cd changes directories, mkdir creates folders, pwd shows current path.

questions.question: How do you check if Python is installed?
options: python -v
python --version
pip --version
Both A and B
questions.explanation: python --version (or python -V) shows the installed Python version. pip --version shows pip's version, not Python's.

questions.question: The absolute path '/home/user/projects/my-project' is different from relative path './my-project' depending on where you are in the file system.
questions.explanation: Absolute paths (starting with /) always refer to the same location. Relative paths (like .) depend on your current directory.

questions.question: What does 'git commit' do?
options: Uploads code to GitHub
Saves a snapshot of changes in your local repository
Creates a new branch
Deletes a file
questions.explanation: git commit saves changes to your local repo with a message. git push uploads to GitHub. Commit = local save point.

questions.question: Why is SSH better than HTTPS for GitHub authentication?
options: SSH is slower but safer
SSH uses keys instead of passwords; no need to enter credentials each time
SSH works on Windows but HTTPS doesn't
There's no difference
questions.explanation: SSH uses public/private key pairs. HTTPS requires username/password each time. SSH is more convenient and just as secure.

questions.question: You should commit your private SSH key to GitHub for backup.
questions.explanation: NEVER commit private keys anywhere. Keep them local, protected. Only public keys go into GitHub settings.

questions.question: What's the danger of hardcoding an API key in your Python file?
options: It makes the code slower
It exposes the key if the file is committed to GitHub; bots scan and exploit it within minutes
It only works on your machine
No danger; APIs are public anyway
questions.explanation: Public GitHub repos are scanned constantly. Exposed API keys = credit card being used, compute resources stolen, data breach. Always use .env

questions.question: .env files should be committed to GitHub so everyone has the same configuration.
questions.explanation: .env should NEVER be committed. It contains secrets. Use .gitignore to prevent accidental commits. Share .env.example instead.

questions.question: What should you put in .env.example?
options: Your actual API keys and passwords
A template showing what variables are needed, without real values
Nothing; it's not used
A copy of .env encrypted
questions.explanation: .env.example documents which secrets the project needs. Commit this. So others know what to configure, but have no secrets exposed.

questions.question: What is a Docker container?
options: A lightweight virtual machine with your app, OS, and dependencies packaged together
A GitHub repository for Docker files
A way to encrypt your code
A cloud service like AWS
questions.explanation: A container bundles everything needed: OS (lightweight), Python, libraries, code. Makes deployments consistent across machines.

questions.question: You should commit the 'venv' folder (virtual environment) to GitHub.
questions.explanation: Never commit venv, .venv, or Docker images. They're huge and platform-specific. Commit pyproject.toml and Dockerfile instead.

questions.question: What does 'docker build -t my-app:v1 .' do?
options: Runs your app in a container
Uploads your app to Docker Hub
Creates an image (blueprint) named 'my-app' with tag 'v1' from the Dockerfile in the current directory
Downloads an app from Docker Hub
questions.explanation: docker build creates an image (blueprint). docker run executes it. -t names it. . means 'use Dockerfile in current folder.'

questions.question: Which of these is NOT a good reason to use environment variables for API keys?
options: Secrets aren't exposed in code or logs
You can use different keys in dev vs production without code changes
Environment variables are cheaper than APIs
Sharing code is safer because secrets aren't in the repository
questions.explanation: Environment variables improve security and configuration, not cost. Never commit to repo without protecting secrets first.

questions.question: OpenAI, Google Gemini, and Anthropic Claude have identical API interfaces.
questions.explanation: Different providers have different APIs (different endpoints, parameters, response formats). But high-level patterns are similar.

questions.question: What's the first thing you should do before making API calls in production?
options: Call the API as many times as possible to test
Set usage limits and billing alerts to avoid unexpected charges
Publish your code to GitHub immediately
Nothing; just go ahead
questions.explanation: APIs can be expensive. Always set limits and alerts FIRST, then test carefully. Unexpected bills are no fun.

questions.question: What's the main difference between a script and an agent?
options: Scripts are longer
Agents can decide what to do next based on the situation; scripts follow a fixed sequence
Agents are faster
No difference; they're the same
questions.explanation: Script: 'Do X, then Y, then Z.' Agent: 'Do the task. I'll figure out which tools to use and in what order.'

questions.question: A 'tool' in LangChain is just a Python function that the agent can call.
questions.explanation: Exactly. A tool wraps a function so the agent knows: name, description, what it does. Then the agent decides if/when to use it.

questions.question: When you run an agent with verbose=True, what do you see?
options: More error messages
The thinking process: how the agent decides which tools to use and what it learns
Faster execution
A graphical interface
questions.explanation: verbose=True logs the agent's decision-making in real-time. Super useful for debugging and understanding what it's doing.

questions.question: An agent can only use one tool per execution.
questions.explanation: Agents can use multiple tools in sequence. E.g., get data from Tool A, process it, use Tool B to refine, then answer.
